question: Is there a U.S. Senate race in New Hampshire for the 2026 cycle?
question_variants: Who is running for Jeanne Shaheen's Senate seat?
Is the New Hampshire US Senate seat an open race?
answer: Yes. There is an open U.S. Senate race in New Hampshire in 2026. Incumbent Senator Jeanne Shaheen (D) announced on March 12, 2025, that she will not seek a fourth term. Senator Maggie Hassan is not on the ballot until 2028.
source_name: New Hampshire Secretary of State – Upcoming Elections

question: What is the term length for the Governor of New Hampshire?
question_variants: How often does New Hampshire elect a Governor?
Is the NH Governor up for election every two years?
answer: The Governor of New Hampshire serves a two-year term. New Hampshire is one of only two states (with Vermont) that holds gubernatorial elections in every even-numbered year under N.H. Const. Pt. 2, Art. 42.

question: Is Governor Kelly Ayotte running for re-election in 2026?
question_variants: Is the New Hampshire Governor's seat open in 2026?
Can Kelly Ayotte seek a second term?
answer: Yes. Governor Kelly Ayotte (R), who was elected to her first term in 2024, is eligible for re-election in 2026. New Hampshire has no term limits for the office of Governor.
source_name: New Hampshire Secretary of State – Candidates

question: What is the role of the New Hampshire Executive Council in approving appointments?
question_variants: What can the Executive Council approve?
Checking the power of the NH Governor
answer: The Executive Council is a five-member elected body that checks the Governor's power. Under N.H. Const. Pt. 2, Art. 47, the Governor and Council have a mutual 'negative' on each other, meaning a 3-2 Council majority is required to approve contracts over $10,000, pardons, and gubernatorial appointments.
scope: Statewide candidates and Executive Council candidates

question: Does New Hampshire have a Lieutenant Governor?
question_variants: Is there a Lt. Governor on the NH ballot?
answer: No. New Hampshire does not have an office of Lieutenant Governor. Under N.H. Const. Pt. 2, Art. 49, the President of the Senate is the first in the line of succession to perform the duties of the Governor.

question: How is the Attorney General selected in New Hampshire?
question_variants: Is the NH Attorney General elected?
Who picks the Attorney General in New Hampshire?
answer: The Attorney General of New Hampshire is appointed by the Governor with the advice and consent of the Executive Council. The AG is not popularly elected by the voters and serves a four-year term per N.H. Const. Pt. 2, Art. 46 and RSA 7:1.
source_name: RSA 7:1 and N.H. Const. Pt. 2, Art. 46

question: How is the New Hampshire Secretary of State chosen?
question_variants: Is the NH Secretary of State an elected office?
Who chooses the Secretary of State in NH?
Joint ballot election for NH SoS
answer: The Secretary of State is elected by a joint ballot of the New Hampshire House and Senate (the General Court) rather than by popular vote. This election occurs at the start of each new legislative session under N.H. Const. Pt. 2, Art. 67.

question: How is the New Hampshire State Treasurer selected?
question_variants: Is there an election for State Treasurer in NH?
Who picks the NH State Treasurer?
Appointment vs election for NH Treasurer
answer: Similar to the Secretary of State, the New Hampshire State Treasurer is elected by a joint ballot of the House of Representatives and the Senate under N.H. Const. Pt. 2, Art. 67, and does not appear on the popular ballot for voters.

question: What is the size of the New Hampshire House of Representatives?
question_variants: How many members are in the NH House?
Composition of the NH General Court
answer: The New Hampshire House of Representatives consists of 400 members, making it the third-largest legislative body in the English-speaking world. Under N.H. Const. Pt. 2, Art. 9, all 400 seats are up for election every two years.

question: What is the size of the New Hampshire State Senate?
question_variants: How many state senators does NH have?
answer: The New Hampshire State Senate consists of 24 members representing 24 districts. Under N.H. Const. Pt. 2, Art. 25, senators are elected to two-year terms concurrently with the House.

question: What are the term lengths for New Hampshire state legislators?
question_variants: How long is a NH House term?
answer: Both New Hampshire State Representatives and State Senators serve two-year terms. Every seat in both the 400-member House and the 24-member Senate is up for election in every even-numbered year.
source_name: N.H. Const. Pt. 2, Art. 9 and Art. 25

question: What is the annual salary for New Hampshire state legislators?
question_variants: How much are NH House members paid?
Is the NH legislature a part-time job?
answer: New Hampshire legislators are the lowest-paid in the U.S. Under N.H. Const. Pt. 2, Art. 15, members receive $200 per biennial term ($100/year). Presiding officers receive $250. Mileage is limited to 45 legislative days; special sessions add $3/day for up to 15 days.

question: When is the 2026 New Hampshire State Primary Election?
question_variants: What date is the NH primary in 2026?
answer: The 2026 New Hampshire State Primary Election is scheduled for Tuesday, September 8, 2026. This partisan primary is distinct from the earlier presidential primary and is held on the second Tuesday of September per RSA 653:7.

question: What is the 'First-in-the-Nation' primary mandate in New Hampshire?
question_variants: How is the NH primary date determined?
answer: Under RSA 653:9, New Hampshire's presidential primary must be held at least seven days before any 'similar election' in any other state. This statutory mandate requires the Secretary of State to set a date that ensures New Hampshire remains the first primary in the nation.

question: Who has the authority to set the date for the New Hampshire primary?
question_variants: Can the Secretary of State move the NH primary date?
Who controls the NH election calendar?
answer: The New Hampshire Secretary of State has the sole authority to set the date of the presidential primary to comply with the first-in-the-nation mandate. For the state primary in September, the date is fixed by statute (RSA 653:7) unless a conflict arises with specific holidays.

question: What are the individual and corporate contribution limits for state candidates in New Hampshire?
question_variants: How much can an individual give to a candidate in NH?
answer: Under RSA 664:4, individuals and corporations may contribute up to $15,000 per candidate per election cycle. This limit was increased by 2023 legislative reforms (SB 140) and applies uniformly to persons and entities.

question: What are the contribution limits for political committees and parties in New Hampshire?
question_variants: How much can I give to a political party in New Hampshire?
answer: Individuals and businesses may contribute up to $30,000 per election cycle to a political committee or a political party. These caps are governed by RSA 664:4, V, and are distinct from limits on direct contributions to candidates.

question: What is a Political Advocacy Organization (PAO) and what are its contribution limits?
question_variants: Can PAOs give unlimited money to candidates?
answer: A Political Advocacy Organization (PAO) is a registered entity that can accept unlimited contributions from donors. Under RSA 664:4, V, a PAO may also make unlimited direct contributions to other committees, including candidate committees, effectively allowing for unlimited pass-through fundraising.

question: Are direct corporate contributions to candidates legal in New Hampshire?
question_variants: Can a business use treasury funds to donate in NH?
answer: Yes. While specific older statutes still contain restrictive language, direct corporate treasury contributions are permitted in New Hampshire up to the individual limit of $15,000 per cycle. This follows federal court rulings and the 2023 consolidation of limits under RSA 664:4.

question: Is the Online Campaign Finance System (OCFS) mandatory for candidates in New Hampshire?
question_variants: Do I have to file my NH campaign reports online?
Electronic filing requirements for NH State House
answer: Online filing is mandatory for Governor, Executive Council, and State Senate candidates under RSA 664:9-a. It will become mandatory for State House and all other elective offices, including county positions, as of November 25, 2026.

question: Is a photo ID required to vote in person in New Hampshire?
question_variants: Can I vote in NH without a driver's license?
answer: Yes. Voters must present a valid photo ID to vote in person under RSA 659:13. Valid IDs include NH driver's licenses, U.S. passports, military IDs, and student IDs from NH schools that meet specific criteria.

question: Does New Hampshire allow same-day voter registration?
question_variants: Can I register to vote at the polls in NH?
How late can I register to vote in NH?
answer: Yes. New Hampshire allows eligible citizens to register and vote on the same day at their polling place. Voters must provide proof of identity, citizenship, age, and domicile at the time of registration per RSA 654:7-a.

question: Does New Hampshire have no-excuse early voting?
question_variants: When can I vote early in NH?
Does NH have a general early voting period?
Absentee vs early voting in New Hampshire
answer: No. New Hampshire does not offer no-excuse early voting. Voters must cast their ballot in person on Election Day, unless they meet the statutory requirements to request an absentee ballot.
source_name: New Hampshire Secretary of State – Absentee Voting

question: How are judges selected in New Hampshire, and are there retirement rules?
question_variants: Who appoints Supreme Court justices in NH?
answer: NH judges are appointed by the Governor and confirmed by a majority vote of the Executive Council under N.H. Const. Pt. 2, Art. 46. The system includes the 5-justice Supreme Court and Superior/District courts. All judges face mandatory retirement at age 70 per N.H. Const. Pt. 2, Art. 78.
source_name: N.H. Const. Pt. 2, Art. 46 and Art. 78

question: Can voters recall state officials or judges in New Hampshire?
question_variants: How to remove a NH state legislator
Does New Hampshire have a recall by petition?
answer: No. New Hampshire does not have a constitutional or statutory mechanism for the recall of statewide officials, legislators, or judges by voter petition. Removal is only possible via the legislative impeachment process or specific disciplinary actions.

question: What are the residency and age requirements for New Hampshire U.S. Senate candidates?
question_variants: How long to live in NH for Senate?
answer: Under the U.S. Constitution Article I § 3, a candidate must be at least 30 years old, a U.S. citizen for nine years, and an inhabitant of New Hampshire at the time of the election.
source_name: New Hampshire Secretary of State – Running for Office

question: What are the residency and age requirements for New Hampshire U.S. House candidates?
question_variants: How long to live in NH for US House?
answer: Per the U.S. Constitution Article I § 2, a candidate must be at least 25 years old, a U.S. citizen for seven years, and an inhabitant of New Hampshire when elected. Residency within the specific congressional district is not a constitutional requirement.
source_name: New Hampshire Secretary of State – Running for Office

question: What are the inhabitant and age requirements for the New Hampshire State Senate?
answer: Under N.H. Const. Pt. 2, Art. 29, State Senate candidates must be at least 30 years old and have been an inhabitant of New Hampshire for seven years immediately preceding the election. They must also be an inhabitant of the district they represent when elected.

question: What are the inhabitant and age requirements for the New Hampshire State House of Representatives?
answer: Under N.H. Const. Pt. 2, Art. 14, State House candidates must be at least 18 years old and have been an inhabitant of New Hampshire for two years. They must also be an inhabitant of the town, ward, or district they represent when elected.

question: What are the residency and age requirements for the New Hampshire Executive Council?
answer: Executive Council candidates must be at least 30 years old, a registered voter, and an inhabitant of New Hampshire for at least seven years preceding the election. They must also be a resident of the district they seek to represent.

question: Is Senator Maggie Hassan's seat up for election in 2026?
question_variants: When is Maggie Hassan up for re-election?
NH Senate seats on the 2026 ballot
Is both NH Senate seats open in 2026?
answer: No. Senator Maggie Hassan (D) was re-elected in 2022 to a six-year term. Her seat is not on the ballot for the 2026 cycle; she is next up for re-election in 2028.
source_name: New Hampshire Secretary of State – Upcoming Elections

question: What is the order of succession if the Governor of New Hampshire is unable to serve?
question_variants: Who takes over if the Governor resigns NH?
answer: Under N.H. Const. Pt. 2, Art. 49, if the Governor's office becomes vacant, the President of the Senate assumes the duties. If the Senate President is also unable to serve, the Speaker of the House is next in the line of succession.

question: What are the filing deadlines for the 2026 partisan primary in New Hampshire?
question_variants: When to file for NH office?
answer: The candidate filing window for the September 8, 2026 partisan primary typically opens on the first Wednesday in June and closes on the second Friday. For the 2026 cycle, this period is approximately June 3 to June 12, 2026, per RSA 655:14.

question: What is the filing deadline for independent candidates in the 2026 New Hampshire general election?
question_variants: When to file nomination papers NH?
answer: Independent or non-party candidates must file their nomination papers and declarations of candidacy no later than 5:00 p.m. on the Wednesday one week before the state primary. For the 2026 cycle, the deadline is September 2, 2026, per RSA 655:43.

question: What is a 'challenged ballot' in New Hampshire election law?
question_variants: Voting if your identity is challenged NH
answer: New Hampshire uses a 'challenged ballot' mechanism under RSA 659:12. If a voter's eligibility is questioned, they may still vote after signing a challenged voter affidavit. Their ballot is marked and segregated for potential review if the election result is contested.

question: How are voter registration lists maintained in New Hampshire?
answer: Voter lists are maintained by municipal supervisors of the checklist. Under HAVA and state law, lists are updated following each election. Every ten years, supervisors must conduct a mandatory review and verification of the entire checklist to remove ineligible entries.
source_name: New Hampshire Secretary of State – Voter Registration

question: What are the rules for college student voting domicile in New Hampshire?
question_variants: Can out-of-state students vote in NH?
answer: Following the enactment of HB 1264, any person who votes in New Hampshire establishes 'domicile.' For students, this means that while they may vote, they become subject to New Hampshire laws regarding motor vehicle registration and driver's licensing.

question: Is a witness signature required on New Hampshire absentee ballots?
question_variants: Does someone have to sign my mail ballot envelope?
answer: No. New Hampshire does not require a witness signature or a notary for an absentee ballot to be counted. The voter's identity is verified by comparing the signature on the affidavit envelope with the signature on the absentee ballot application.
source_name: New Hampshire Secretary of State – Absentee Voting

question: What is the donor itemization threshold for occupation and employer reporting in New Hampshire?
question_variants: When to list employer for campaign gifts NH
answer: Under the 2024 legislative reform (HB 1380), the threshold for disclosing a contributor's occupation and employer was increased. Campaigns must now itemize this information for any individual who contributes more than $1,000 in aggregate during an election cycle.

question: What is the threshold for public disclosure of contributor identifying information in New Hampshire?
answer: Under 2024 reforms and the Right-to-Know exemption (RSA 91-A), the name and address of an individual contributor are subject to public disclosure only if their aggregate contributions exceed $200 during the election cycle.

question: What is the threshold for reporting an independent expenditure in New Hampshire?
question_variants: When to file an IE report NH
answer: Any person or political committee that spends more than $1,000 in aggregate on an independent expenditure must file a disclosure report with the Secretary of State under RSA 664:13. If the expenditure exceeds $5,000 within 24 hours, a report must be filed within 24 hours.

question: What is the 'Paid for by' attribution requirement for political advertising in New Hampshire?
question_variants: Who paid for this ad disclaimer NH
answer: Under RSA 664:14, almost all political advertisements—including print, broadcast, and electronic messages—must clearly and conspicuously state the name and address of the person or committee who paid for and authorized the communication.

question: How do voluntary spending limits work in New Hampshire state elections?
answer: Under RSA 664:5-a, candidates for Governor, Executive Council, or State Senate who voluntarily agree to a specific campaign spending cap are exempt from both the filing fee and the signature petition requirements for ballot access.

question: What is the anonymous campaign contribution cap in New Hampshire?
question_variants: Can I donate cash anonymously in NH?
answer: Under RSA 664:4-b, anonymous campaign contributions are strictly prohibited. Any anonymous contribution received must be delivered within 48 hours to the State Treasurer for deposit into the general fund.

question: What are 'floterial districts' in the New Hampshire House of Representatives?
question_variants: How are NH House seats allocated?
answer: Floterial districts are a unique New Hampshire redistricting feature where a single legislative seat 'floats' over multiple towns or wards to satisfy population equality. This allows for proportional representation in areas where town populations do not neatly align with a single-seat quota.

question: Does New Hampshire have a single-subject rule for state legislation?
answer: No. New Hampshire is one of the few states that does not have a constitutional or statutory single-subject rule for legislation. This permits the passage of omnibus bills that contain multiple, unrelated topics in a single legislative act.

question: How are constitutional amendments referred to the voters in New Hampshire?
answer: New Hampshire has the highest ratification threshold in the nation. Under N.H. Const. Pt. 2, Art. 100, an amendment must be passed by a 3/5 majority in both the House and Senate, then ratified by at least 2/3 of the voters in a general election.

question: What is the traditional 'Town Meeting Day' in New Hampshire?
answer: Annual Town Meeting is traditionally held on the second Tuesday of March for the vast majority of New Hampshire towns. This day is used for direct democracy, where voters approve budgets, tax rates, and elect town officials.

question: How often is the question of a constitutional convention put to New Hampshire voters?
question_variants: When is the next NH convention question?
answer: Under N.H. Const. Pt. 2, Art. 100, a question asking voters whether a convention should be called to revise or amend the state constitution must appear on the general election ballot every 10 years (e.g., 2022, 2032).

question: When are the pre-election campaign finance reports due in New Hampshire?
answer: Candidates must file primary and general election disclosure reports with the Secretary of State on the 20th day before the election and the 8th day before the election, as well as a post-election report on the 10th day after the election.

question: Does New Hampshire have rules regarding the personal use of campaign funds?
question_variants: Can I use campaign money for my mortgage NH?
Personal use prohibition NH campaign finance
answer: Yes. Under RSA 664:4-c, campaign contributions must be used for expenses directly related to the election. Personal use, such as paying for a candidate's personal mortgage, rent, or luxury items, is strictly prohibited.

question: How many signatures are required for independent candidates for Governor or U.S. Senator in New Hampshire?
question_variants: Nomination paper signatures for statewide office NH
answer: Under RSA 655:42, an independent (non-party) candidate for Governor or U.S. Senator must collect at least 3,000 valid signatures on nomination papers from registered voters to qualify for the general election ballot.

question: What is the role of the New Hampshire Ballot Law Commission?
question_variants: Who decides ballot access disputes in NH?
answer: The Ballot Law Commission is a bipartisan body under RSA 665 that hears and decides all disputes regarding candidate qualifications, nomination petitions, and the wording of ballot questions.

question: Does New Hampshire require inaugural committees to file financial reports?
answer: Yes. While not candidate committees, gubernatorial inaugural committees must register with the Secretary of State and file a financial disclosure report within 90 days after the inaugural activities are completed.
source_name: New Hampshire Secretary of State – Campaign Finance

question: Are candidates required to appoint a campaign treasurer in New Hampshire?
question_variants: Who can be a campaign treasurer in NH?
answer: Yes. Under RSA 664:6, every candidate for state or county office who receives or spends funds must appoint a campaign treasurer. The name and address of the treasurer must be filed with the Secretary of State before any financial activity occurs.

question: What is the penalty for filing a campaign finance report late in New Hampshire?
answer: Under RSA 664:20, the Secretary of State can assess a fine of $10 per day for each day a campaign report remains delinquent. Repeated or willful failures to file can result in criminal prosecution.

question: What are the rules regarding voter registration deadlines in New Hampshire?
question_variants: Last day to register by mail in NH
answer: Voters can register in person at their town or city clerk's office up to approximately 6 to 13 days before an election (varies by municipality). Additionally, New Hampshire offers Election Day Registration at all polling places.

question: Does New Hampshire law allow time off for employees to vote?
question_variants: Do I get paid to vote in New Hampshire?
answer: No. New Hampshire is one of the few states that does not have a statute requiring employers to grant time off (paid or unpaid) for employees to vote. Employees must generally coordinate with their employers or utilize absentee voting if eligible.

question: What are the criminal penalties for voter intimidation in New Hampshire?
question_variants: Is voter intimidation illegal in NH?
answer: Under RSA 659:40, using force, threats, or intimidation to influence a person to vote or refrain from voting, or to interfere with the free exercise of the franchise, is a misdemeanor. If the act involves a public official, it may be elevated.

question: How are vacancies in the New Hampshire General Court filled?
answer: Vacancies in the House of Representatives or State Senate are filled by special election. The Governor and Executive Council must issue a precept for a special election within a specific timeframe after the vacancy occurs under RSA 661:8.

question: How are vacancies in the New Hampshire Executive Council filled?
answer: If an Executive Council seat becomes vacant, a special election must be held. The Governor and the remaining members of the Council issue a precept setting the date for the election to fill the unexpired term per RSA 661:5.

question: How are vacancies in New Hampshire county offices filled?
answer: Vacancies in elected county offices (Sheriff, Attorney, Treasurer, etc.) are filled by an appointment from the Superior Court judges for that county. The appointee serves until the next general election.

question: What is an 'SB 2' town in New Hampshire?
answer: An 'SB 2' town (named after Senate Bill 2) uses the official ballot voting system under RSA 40:13. These towns have a deliberative session to discuss warrant articles, followed by a subsequent day of traditional booth voting on the articles, instead of the traditional open Town Meeting.

question: What is the term length for elected county officials in New Hampshire?
question_variants: How long is a NH Sheriff's term?
answer: All elected county officers in New Hampshire—including Sheriff, County Attorney, Treasurer, Register of Deeds, Register of Probate, and County Commissioners—serve two-year terms and are elected in partisan contests in even-numbered years.

question: What is an 'Assent to Candidacy' in New Hampshire ballot access?
answer: When candidates utilize the primary petition signature alternative for ballot access, they must also file an official 'Assent to Candidacy' under RSA 655:25, formally declaring they are qualified for the office and will accept the nomination.

question: What is the 'last day' in-person filing requirement in New Hampshire?
question_variants: Can I mail my NH candidate filing on the last day?
answer: Under RSA 655:43, any candidate or person submitting nomination papers or declarations on the final day of the filing period must deliver them in person to the filing officer by 5:00 p.m. Filings mailed and arriving on the last day may be rejected if not received by the deadline.

question: What is the electioneering buffer zone distance at New Hampshire polling places?
question_variants: Where to stand at the polls NH
answer: New Hampshire enforces a 10-foot electioneering buffer zone measured from the entrance to the building containing the polling place. Under RSA 659:43, no person may electioneer or distribute campaign materials within this specific corridor.

question: What are the rules regarding candidate loans to their own campaigns in New Hampshire?
question_variants: Can I lend my campaign money NH?
answer: Candidates may make unlimited personal loans to their own campaigns in New Hampshire. However, these loans must be formally documented as debt in campaign finance reports and must be repaid using legal campaign contributions.

question: Is out-of-state PAC registration required in New Hampshire?
question_variants: NH out of state committee rules
Reporting for national PACs in NH
answer: Yes. Any out-of-state or national political committee that intends to spend more than $1,000 in a New Hampshire election must formally register as a political committee with the Secretary of State and adhere to NH's contribution and reporting rules.
